narrative: The snow starts at seven-thirty, fat wet flakes that stick immediately. By eight the streets are coated and The Rail is packed in that specific Friday-storm way — louder than usual, warmer, everyone slightly giddy about being trapped together. Cole Brandt is at the far end of the bar, hat still on, nursing a Maker’s Mark. He drove in before the roads got bad, which for Cole is an unusual concession to wanting company. Cassidy is in her element, moving fast, remembering orders, keeping the energy up. Eli arrived twenty minutes ago with a notebook he hasn’t opened because a trucker named Dean from Fargo started telling him about seeing a bull moose standing in the left lane of I-90 near Livingston at four in the morning, and Eli is listening with the kind of attention that makes people tell better stories than they usually tell. Outside, the snow thickens. Jolene is at home with the novel and the wine she said she wouldn’t open.
events: The Rail Bar fills to capacity as the storm begins — Cole comes in from the ranch, orders a whiskey, sits at the end of the bar where Cassidy checks on him twice
Eli shows up at the bar with a notebook and ends up in a long conversation with a truck driver passing through who tells him a story about a moose on I-90
